protocol: medical - respiratory distress/asthma/copd/croup/reactive airway (respiratory distress - croup/epiglottits) (protocol 9 - 11)

dialogue.utterance: Unit 2 to Huguenot High School for a breathing problem.
Hi, I'm Mark. What made you call 911 today?
I'm having trouble breathing.
Can you tell me your name?
Your eyes are open, and you are looking directly at me.
You're answering questions coherently and appropriately.
You can follow instructions and move your body on command.
Can you take a deep breath and speak clearly right now?
Your airway is clear and maintainable-no signs of obstruction or stridor.
Your breathing is fast but with normal depth.
I don't see cyanosis, asymmetry, or labored breathing at this time.
No use of accessory muscles is observed right now.
Pulse ox is showing 93% with oxygen support currently on.
Let me feel your pulse. It's strong and regular.
Your skin is warm, dry, and appears normal in color.
Your heart rate is 78 beats per minute.
Breathing is 28 per minute-proceeding with further checks.
Blood pressure is 105 over 64 mmHg.
Oxygen saturation is 93%-ensuring supplemental oxygen is in place.
EKG shows you are in sinus rhythm without noted ST abnormalities.
When did your breathing issue start today?
It began in the morning, around 0600 hours.
What did you try to manage your symptoms after it started?
I used my albuterol, but it didn't improve much, so I came to the nurse's office at school.
Do you have any medical conditions that you would like to mention?
I have a history of moderate asthma and anemia in my blood profile.
What medications are you currently taking for your conditions?
I use albuterol, Singulair, and Advair for my respiratory needs every day.
Any drug allergies that we should know about for your profile?
I don't have any known allergies to any medications or products.
When was the last time you had any food or drink prior to calling 911 just now?
I had breakfast at 0600, the same time I noticed my breathing issue began.
Did you notice any precipitating activity or change in routine before the morning incident started now?
No, everything was normal prior to the issues-just routine activities.
Please confirm your age and weight for transport documentation purposes.
I am 16 years old, and I weigh about 59 kilograms.
I'm going to listen to your lungs now. Call out anything unusual such as wheezing or crackles.
I can detect expiratory wheezing in both the upper and lower left and right lung field areas-consistently bilateral.
Your skin is still warm, dry, and you have good capillary refill.
Capillary refill is less than two seconds-suggesting good perfusion at present time.
Can you feel discomfort or tightness in your neck region? It's part of our survey.
No signs of stiffness, masses, or swollen lymph nodes are found during visual or tactile evaluation now.
Your ears appear clear, with no discharge or visible obstructions.
No oral obstruction noted-your throat and airway are intact without swelling or foreign substances detected.
Heart sounds are clear and regular, no murmur or arrhythmia detected.
Let me check for abdominal tenderness or distension now-has your stomach felt normal?
Bowel sounds are not easily heard today, but you do not report any abdominal pain.
I will now assess peripheral pulses in your hands and feet for blood flow.
You move all limbs with no pain or deformity present. Peripheries are warm with regular pulse strength.
Let me run a scan for rashes, bruises, or tumors on your skin. Are you okay with that?
I see no evidence of rashes, bites, or wounds on your skin at the moment.
Blood sugar is currently 111 mg/dL as tested at this time.
Tympanic temperature is 100.9 degrees Fahrenheit-suggesting mild fever but not currently impacting respirations.
End-tidal CO2 is 20 mmHg with a square waveform - consistent with effective breathing.
I'll now prepare you for four-lead ECG application for continuous cardiac monitoring.
A 12-lead ECG will be placed now to get a comprehensive look at your cardiac activity.
I'll start a DuoNeb nebulizer now for targeted bronchodilation.
You'll receive 5 mg of albuterol mixed with 0.5 mg ipratropium together in the nebulization.
The nebulizer is being used with oxygen at 8 liters per minute right now to increase respiratory oxygenation.
I will now give you 10 mg of Dexamethasone in pill form to reduce airway inflammation.
I'm going to attempt to start an IV in your left arm for potential administration later. Please brace up.
The IV attempt is unsuccessful after one try-we'll use oral medications instead.
Your heart rate has increased to 91 now-recorded at this time.
Your breathing has slowed down, from 28 to 18 respirations per minute.
Blood pressure has increased to 165 over 91 mmHg-note that elevated systolic.
Your oxygen saturation has improved from 93% to 100%-continue with current oxygen flow.
EKG remains consistent-still in sinus rhythm as before treatments began.
I'll confirm there's no change in your level of consciousness or mental responsiveness at this time.
Your wheezing is still present-nothing new post-treatment thus far in this regard.
Breathing pattern and efficiency are stable-nothing has worsened or required additional intervention.
We will now follow protocol 9 for respiratory distress due to asthma exacerbation.
We are heading to [COMPANY_NAME]'s Pediatric Emergency Department for further assessment.
Let me secure you to the stretcher with a five-point harness to ensure safe transport.
The transport is non-emergent based on vital stability and clinical assessment.
I don't have any chest or abdominal pain that I've noticed so far.
I haven't been vomiting or feeling nauseous up until this point today.
I don't feel lightheaded or dizzy either since the episode began this morning.
You are securely fastened and ready for transport.
[COMPANY_NAME] Pediatric Emergency Department is the confirmed destination hospital as part of transfer.
I will provide a detailed report upon arrival to the receiving nurse for continuity of care.
Medications, procedures, and all documentation have been prepared and will be submitted accurately.
Breathing sounds remain characterized by wheezing bilaterally as noted prior in upper/lower lung fields.
You are safely and comfortably transported in five-point harness, fully supported for in-transit care.
Reporting full transfer of care with precise documentation of all data, vitals, and response to administered treatments.
The pediatric emergency department at [CITY] has been contacted and is ready for our arrival with full info.
Patient is being transferred now to [COMPANY_NAME] for pediatric evaluation and advanced care.
I am delivering the patient directly to the receiving nurse in ED Bed 9 at this pediatric facility upon arrival.
